facts_disclosed: Uses Long An Premium feed 380,000 dong/25kg

turns.content: friend, feed price keep going up. every month more expensive. i need calculate how much i spend per harvest so i know if i still make money, you know

That's a smart thing to calculate — knowing your exact feed cost per harvest is the only way to know if you're really profitable or just working for nothing. I can help you work through the numbers.

Let's start with the basics. What feed are you using, how much does it cost, and roughly how many bags do you go through per harvest cycle?
i use Long An Premium. 380,000 dong for 25 kilo bag. good feed, shrimp grow well on it. but last year was 340,000 dong same bag. go up 40,000 in one year, you know

That's about a 12% price increase in one year — that's steep. Long An Premium is a decent feed but that kind of annual increase eats into your margins fast.

Okay, so 380,000 dong per 25kg bag. Now let me help you calculate the full picture:

1. How many bags do you use per pond per harvest cycle?
2. How long is one cycle — from stocking to harvest?
3. What's your typical yield in kilos at harvest?

Once we have those numbers, we can figure out your feed cost per kilo of shrimp produced, and compare that to your selling price.

okay. one cycle about 100 to 110 day for vannamei. big pond maybe use 80 bag per cycle. small pond maybe 35 bag each. that is 150 bag total for all 3 pond. 150 time 380,000... you calculate for me friend. math not my strong thing

No problem, let me run those numbers:

**Feed cost per cycle (all 3 ponds):**
- 150 bags x 380,000 dong = **57,000,000 dong** per harvest cycle

Breaking it down:
- Big pond (80 bags): 30,400,000 dong
- Small pond 1 (35 bags): 13,300,000 dong
- Small pond 2 (35 bags): 13,300,000 dong

That's 57 million dong just on feed every ~100 days. Now the important question — what's your typical total yield from all 3 ponds? If we know that, we can figure out your feed cost per kilo of shrimp, which tells you whether the numbers still work.
good harvest, all 3 pond together maybe 5 ton. 5000 kilo. sometime less, maybe 4200 if have problem. 5 ton is when everything go right, you know

Okay, let's work it out both ways:

**Good harvest (5,000 kg):**
- Feed cost per kilo: 57,000,000 / 5,000 = **11,400 dong/kg**

**Tough harvest (4,200 kg):**
- Feed cost per kilo: 57,000,000 / 4,200 = **13,571 dong/kg**

Now feed is usually about 50-60% of total production cost for vannamei. So your total cost is probably around 20,000-25,000 dong per kilo.

What are you selling at? If you're getting somewhere around 150,000-170,000 dong per kilo, your margins are still healthy. But that 12% feed increase every year — if it keeps going, in a few years it really starts to squeeze.

Does this match what you feel about the economics? Are there other big costs besides feed?

sell price depend on season but yes around 160,000 to 170,000 per kilo for good size shrimp. 30 gram each or bigger. smaller one less money. other cost, yes. electricity for aerator, that big cost. worker salary. seed shrimp. medicine if shrimp get sick. but feed is biggest one

Right, so at 165,000 dong/kg average and a feed cost of about 11,400-13,500/kg, you've still got a good margin. But feed being the biggest cost means that's where price increases hurt the most.

Let me put it in perspective:
- Last year at 340,000/bag, your feed cost was: 150 bags x 340,000 = **51,000,000 dong** per cycle
- This year: **57,000,000 dong**
- That's **6,000,000 dong more** per cycle just from the feed price increase

That's basically 6 million dong taken right out of your profit every 100 days. Over a year with maybe 3 cycles, that's 18 million dong less in your pocket.

Have you looked at other feed brands? Sometimes there are comparable feeds at lower prices. Or have you tried negotiating with the Long An supplier for a bulk discount?

i try talk to supplier. he say price is price, same for everybody. some neighbor use cheaper brand but their shrimp not grow as fast, you know. i see with my own eye. cheap feed, small shrimp. i learn this from experience. 40 year doing this. nobody need tell me what work and what not work

40 years is a serious amount of experience — that's basically a lifetime of watching what actually works versus what people claim works. And you're right, you can see the results with your own eyes. Cheap feed that produces smaller, slower-growing shrimp isn't actually saving you money. The cost per kilo of shrimp produced might actually be higher with cheap feed if the yield drops.

Here's a way to think about it: if cheap feed costs 280,000/bag but your yield drops 20%, you're actually paying more per kilo of shrimp. The math only works if you look at the full picture.

So sticking with Long An Premium might be the right call even at 380,000. The question is whether there's another premium-quality feed that's priced a bit lower, not a cheap brand but a competitive one.

With 40 years of experience, did you learn all this through trial and error, or did you have someone teaching you early on?

my father teach me. he have pond before me, you know. and i watch, i learn. i only go to school 5 year. primary school. then i work. but shrimp, water, weather — this i know better than any book, friend. 40 year. every day. that is my school

Five years of school and forty years on the water — honestly, that's the kind of education that no university can give. Book knowledge is useful but it can't replace someone who's been reading their ponds every single day for four decades. Your father passed down real knowledge and you built on it with your own experience.

And clearly it works — 3 ponds, 5 tons per harvest, consistent quality shrimp. The numbers don't lie.

Back to the feed cost problem — here's my suggestion. You can't control the feed price, but you can try to optimize how much feed gets wasted:

1. **Feed tray monitoring** — if you're not already using feeding trays, they help you dial in the exact amount so you're not overfeeding.
2. **Feeding frequency** — some farmers find that 3-4 smaller feedings per day produce better FCR (feed conversion ratio) than 2 big ones.
3. **Water quality** — shrimp absorb feed better in clean, well-oxygenated water. Every improvement in water quality is basically free feed efficiency.

Even a 5-10% improvement in feed conversion could save you 3-6 million dong per cycle. That would offset most of the price increase.

feed tray i use already. 4 tray in big pond, 2 in each small one. i check after 2 hour like you say, you know. that part i know. but the 3 or 4 feeding per day, that is interesting. i do 2 time now. morning and evening

Good, you're already doing the tray monitoring — that's the most important part. The switch from 2 to 3-4 feedings is worth trying. Here's why it works:

When you give a large meal twice a day, the shrimp can't eat it all before some of it dissolves and breaks apart in the water. That dissolved feed is pure waste. But if you give the same total amount split into 3 or 4 smaller portions, the shrimp eat a higher percentage before it breaks down.

Many farmers who switch from 2 to 4 feedings see a 10-15% improvement in feed conversion. With your numbers:
- 10% less feed waste = about 15 fewer bags per cycle
- 15 bags x 380,000 dong = **5,700,000 dong saved** per cycle

The downside is someone has to be at the ponds more times per day to feed. But if you're already there checking things, maybe it's just adding a midday and late afternoon feeding. Could your workers handle the extra two feedings?

worker can do afternoon one. midday i can do myself, i eat lunch near pond anyway, you know. 5,700,000 dong per cycle, that is good money. almost pay for one worker salary. i think about this. maybe try on small pond first, see what happen

That's exactly the right approach — try it on one small pond first, keep the other small pond on the old schedule as a comparison. After one cycle you'll have real numbers from your own ponds, not someone else's theory. Run it for 100 days, compare the feed used and the yield between the two small ponds. Your own eyes, your own data.

If it works on the small pond, then scale it to all three. If it doesn't, you've only risked one pond.

With 40 years of experience, you know better than anyone that the proof is in the pond. Let me know how it goes.

yes friend. proof is in the pond, i like that. you know, that is true. okay i try this. thank you for calculate the number. my head not good with number but i understand when somebody explain. i go now, have to check aerator before dark

Happy to help with the numbers anytime — that's what I'm here for. You bring the 40 years of pond knowledge, I'll do the multiplication. Good team.

Go check those aerators. And keep an eye on the feed trays when you start the 3-feeding experiment. Good luck!
